page: Amazon Web Services r6id.xlarge is a memory-optimized instance powered by the Nitro hypervisor. It features an x86_64 architecture with an Intel Xeon 8375C processor running at 3.5 GHz. The instance provides 4 dedicated vCPUs derived from 2 physical cores with hyper-threading enabled. It is equipped with 32.0 GB of DDR4 memory running at 3 GHz, translating to 8.0 GB of RAM per core. For local storage, it includes a bundled 237 GB NVMe SSD instance store volume. Baseline network bandwidth is rated at 2 Gbps, and it includes zero complimentary public IPv4 addresses.

Performance benchmarks indicate a mix of average single-core capabilities and weaker multi-core scaling. The stress-ng single-core CPU performance score of 1889.59 and Geekbench single-core score of 1582.0 fall into the average tier. However, multi-core metrics show limitations, with a stress-ng multi-core score of 3848.57 and a Geekbench multi-core score of 3392.0, both categorized as weak. Memory bandwidth is also relatively low, with read-only large block speeds at 29.29 GB/sec and write large block speeds at 24.41 GB/sec. Despite these constraints, the instance achieves a solid static web serving throughput of 6.53 GB/sec and an average PassMark memory score of 2054.36.

From a cost-efficiency perspective, the r6id.xlarge offers a high memory-to-vCPU ratio alongside fast local NVMe storage, making it a viable option for memory-intensive workloads that require low-latency local scratch space without the overhead of external storage. However, the weaker multi-core and database benchmark results suggest it is less suited for heavy parallel processing or high-throughput database engines. It is best utilized for memory-bound applications, single-threaded tasks, caching layers, and lightweight web serving.
description: Amazon Web Services r6id.xlarge is a memory-optimized instance featuring 4 dedicated vCPUs and 32.0 GB of DDR4 RAM, powered by an Intel Xeon 8375C processor at 3.5 GHz. It includes a bundled 237 GB NVMe SSD instance store and operates on the Nitro hypervisor with a baseline network bandwidth of 2 Gbps. Benchmark data shows average single-core performance, with a Geekbench single-core score of 1582.0, but weaker multi-core and memory bandwidth results. The instance is qualitatively cost-efficient for memory-heavy workloads requiring fast local scratch space, though it is limited in highly parallel compute tasks. It is best suited for memory-bound applications, caching layers, and moderate web serving.
